title: diseasenowcasting — AI Agent Reference Guide
text: diseasenowcasting standalone R package Bayesian epidemic nowcasting using RTMB autodiff engine (CppAD + built-Laplace approximation). reimplements diseasenowcast2 Stan-based package Stan/cmdstanr dependency, matching public API: model(likelihood(), epidemic(), delay()). Core idea: reporting delay stochastic process modelled jointly epidemic. likelihood depends observed delays (reporting triangle), censoring handled cleanly. Laplace approximation marginalises latent epidemic coefficients; posterior draws sampled N(mode, H⁻¹) H joint Hessian. Default mode: joint-mode Laplace (use_random = FALSE) — cmdstanr $laplace(). 20–400× faster marginal (random=) Laplace. Two-stage cascade: Stage 1 fits delay (delay_only = TRUE); Stage 2 hard-fixes delay K imputed values pools draws. propagates delay uncertainty without sacrificing convergence.

title: diseasenowcasting — AI Agent Reference Guide
text: diseasenowcasting consumes data tbl_now object companion tbl.now package: tibble carries two time indices (event_date, report_date) plus modelling metadata (strata, covariates, temporal effects, now, units, data type) fully dplyr-compatible. Create Auto-added protected columns: .event_num, .report_num, .delay (= .report_num - .event_num). Removing protected column downgrades object back plain tibble (warning). Getters (every attribute one) Data types — "linelist" (one row per case), "count-incidence" (count reported exactly report_date), "count-cumulative" (cumulative report_date). Convert to_count(x, = "count-incidence"). Temporal effects (lazy, two-step). nowcast(temporal_effects = "auto") , control manually: dplyr verbs (filter/select/mutate/group_by/rename/…) preserve spec never trigger computation; compute_temporal_effects() adds columns. Pre-attach + compute tbl_now nowcast() use covariates; otherwise pass temporal_effects = "none" disable. Modify metadata — changers replace, adders append, removers drop: Utilities tbl.now pitfalls get_event_date() returns column name, dates. calendar grid diseasenowcasting uses nc@engine$min_event + nc@engine$event_unit. Call add_temporal_effects() compute_temporal_effects() (else -op). rowwise() supported tbl_now. get_temporal_effects() returns specs; use get_temporal_effect_cols() names.
code: tbl_now(data, event_date, report_date,         strata = NULL, covariates = NULL, case_count = NULL, is_censored = NULL,         now = NULL,                # Date; default max(report_date)         event_units = "auto",      # "days"|"weeks"|"months"|"years"|"numeric"         report_units = "auto",         data_type = "auto",        # "linelist"|"count-incidence"|"count-cumulative"         t_effects = character(0),  # temporal_effects() spec, stored LAZILY         verbose = TRUE, align_weeks = FALSE) get_event_date(x) / get_report_date(x)    # column NAMES (character), not the dates get_event_units(x) / get_report_units(x)  # "days"|"weeks"|"months"|"years"|"numeric" get_now(x)                                 # Date — the as-of date get_strata(x) / get_num_strata(x) get_covariates(x) / get_num_covariates(x) get_case_count(x) / get_is_censored(x) / get_data_type(x) get_temporal_effects(x)        # list of LAZY specs (length 0 = none attached) get_temporal_effect_cols(x)    # computed column names (character(0) before compute) get_latest_reported_cases(x)   # most-recent count per event_date -> the "truth" for scoring get_initial_reported_cases(x)  # first-reported count per event_date spec <- temporal_effects(day_of_week = TRUE, week_of_year = TRUE,                          month_of_year = FALSE, seasons = integer(0))  # seasons = Fourier periods, e.g. c(7, 52) x <- x |> add_temporal_effects(spec, date_type = "event_date")  # attaches spec; NO columns yet x <- compute_temporal_effects(x)                                # materialises the columns get_temporal_effect_cols(x)                                     # the created column names change_now(x, as.Date("2023-06-01"))           # move the as-of date (re-censors) change_strata(x, ...) / add_strata(x, ...) / remove_strata(x, ...) change_covariates(...) / add_covariates(...) / remove_covariates(...) add_is_censored(x, is_censored = my_logical)   # mark reports as right-censored delays complete_zeroes(x)             # fill missing event/report/strata cells with 0 align_weeks(x, date_col)       # snap to a consistent epiweek day (integer .delay) week_2_date(x, week, year)     # epiweek + year -> Date update(x, new_data)            # bind new rows, preserving attributes is_tbl_now(x) / tbl_now_attributes(x)

code: library(diseasenowcasting) library(dplyr)  # Pre-computed benchmark results (50 evaluation dates per disease), shipped in # inst/extdata.  Look in the installed package first and fall back to the source # tree, so the vignette also knits before the package is installed (e.g. pkgdown # or R CMD build). candidates <- c(   system.file("extdata", "benchmark_scores.rds", package = "diseasenowcasting"),   "../inst/extdata/benchmark_scores.rds",   file.path("inst", "extdata", "benchmark_scores.rds") ) scoring_file <- candidates[nzchar(candidates) & file.exists(candidates)][1]  # Not found locally (e.g. on the pkgdown website)? Download from the public repo. if (is.na(scoring_file)) {   base <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/RodrigoZepeda/diseasenowcasting"   for (ref in c("master", "main")) {                       # whichever is the default branch     url <- paste0(base, "/", ref, "/inst/extdata/benchmark_scores.rds")     tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")     ok  <- tryCatch({       utils::download.file(url, tmp, mode = "wb", quiet = TRUE)       file.exists(tmp) && file.size(tmp) > 0     }, error = function(e) FALSE)     if (isTRUE(ok)) { scoring_file <- tmp; break }   } } have_scores <- !is.na(scoring_file) && file.exists(scoring_file)  if (have_scores) {   scores_df <- readRDS(scoring_file) |>     rename(Model = model, WIS = wis, Overprediction = over, Underprediction = under,            Dispersion = disp, Bias = bias, Cov50 = cov50, Cov90 = cov90, `N dates` = n_dates) }

title: Handling Outlier Delays with Censoring
text: Real surveillance data occasionally contains reports extreme reporting delays. can due typos, healthcare-system hurdles issues related disease’s natural evolution. Colombia’s COVID-19 data (covid_colombia) bulk reports arrive within week two, handful take 100 days: Reporting-delay distribution COVID-19 Colombia (extremes exagerated illustration purposes). reports arrive hundreds days late. parametric delay model (log-normal, gamma, …) fit data containing outlier, extreme value drags estimated delay distribution right. model believes delays longer really , thus inflating recent nowcasts. diseasenowcasting framework offers fix. treats reports right-censored. Instead telling model “case delay exactly 330”, tells “case arrived delay 330” (.e. delay 330 upper bound true delay). follows explain use model automatically detect extreme delays inform model predictions improved.

title: Handling Outlier Delays with Censoring
text: can get new data: update() model. automatically score new data old fit warn something amiss: warning tells us exactly unexpected (reporting delays far longer usual). full table available via extreme_values(): mean_tail_prob expressess probability observing value, cdf_prob probability lying value. title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: vignette derives statistical model underlying diseasenowcasting first principles. intended mathematically oriented readers want understand likelihood construction, epidemic process specifications, inference algorithm.

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: Let \{N_t\}_{t \geq 0} denote latent epidemic process: (unobserved) total number cases occurring event time t. time t reporting-delay process \{D_t\}_{t \geq 0} governs cases eventually appear surveillance data. Formally, N_t = n cases occur time t, case associated delay d_i \sim G_D(\cdot \mid \theta_t), G_D delay’s cummulative distribution function (CDF) parameter vector \theta_t. calendar time \tau \geq t, observe cases whose delay satisfies d_i \leq \tau - t. creates right-censoring: recent event times, cases yet reported. nowcasting problem estimate posterior distribution N_t recent event times, given reported delays current calendar time.

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: Consider single event time t. calendar time \tau \geq t, maximum observable delay d^*_t = \tau - t. Suppose observed k cases delays d_1, \ldots, d_k \leq d^*_t, know n - k cases reported future (delays > d^*_t). Since n unobserved, marginalise n \geq k.

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: joint probability observed delays, conditional N_t = n, : P(d_1, \ldots, d_k \mid \theta_t,\, N_t = n) = \binom{n}{k}   \prod_{=1}^k \Delta G_D(d_i \mid \theta_t)   \cdot \bigl[1 - G_D(d^*_t \mid \theta_t)\bigr]^{n-k}, \Delta G_D(d \mid \theta_t) = \begin{cases}   G_D(d \mid \theta_t) - G_D(d-1 \mid \theta_t) & \text{(continuous } G_D\text{)}, \\   g_D(d \mid \theta_t)                            & \text{(discrete } G_D \text{ pmf } g_D\text{)}. \end{cases} Summing n \geq k incorporating prior \pi(\theta_t), likelihood time t : \text{Likelihood}_t(\theta_t \mid d_1, \ldots, d_k) \;\propto\; \pi(\theta_t) \cdot \prod_{l=0}^{L} \bigl[\Delta G_D(l \mid \theta_t)\bigr]^{m_l} \cdot S_k(\theta_t), m_l = \#\{: d_i = l\} count observed delays equal l, latent process term S_k(\theta_t) = \sum_{n \geq k} \binom{n}{k}   \bigl[1 - G_D(d^*_t \mid \theta_t)\bigr]^{n-k}   P(N_t = n \mid \theta_t).

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: Taking logarithms, per-time contribution \ell_t(\theta_t \mid m_0, m_1, \ldots, m_L) = \ln \pi(\theta_t) + \sum_{l=0}^{L} m_l \ln \Delta G_D(l \mid \theta_t) + \ln S_k(\theta_t). full log-likelihood sums event times: \ell(\theta) = \sum_{t} \ell_t(\theta_t \mid m_0^{(t)}, \ldots, m_L^{(t)}).

what: 2.4 Closed-form S_k for standard epidemic models
title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: Poisson (N_t \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_t)): \ln S_k(\theta_t) = k \ln \lambda_t - \ln k! - G_D(d^*_t \mid \theta_t)\, \lambda_t. Negative Binomial (N_t \sim \mathrm{NB}(r_t, p_t), mean r_t(1-p_t)/p_t): \ln S_k(\theta_t) = k\ln(1-p_t) + r_t \ln p_t + \ln\binom{k + r_t - 1}{k} - (k + r_t)\ln\bigl[p_t + G_D(d^*_t \mid \theta_t)(1-p_t)\bigr]. expressions arise recognising S_k probability-generating function N_t evaluated [1 - G_D(d^*_t)]

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: latent mean incidence time t (across strata s \\{1,\ldots,S\}) \mu_t^{(s)} = \exp\!\bigl(\gamma_0^{(s)} + f^{(s)}(t) + \mathbf{X}_t^{(s)\top} \boldsymbol{\gamma}^{(s)}\bigr), \exp(\cdot) ensures positivity, \gamma_0^{(s)} stratum-specific intercept, \mathbf{X}_t^{(s)} covariate vector (e.g. day--week dummies), f^{(s)}(t) stratum-specific temporal trend. Three specifications f available.

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: Gaussian process f \sim GP(0, k(t,t')) approximated via M eigenfunctions \{\phi_j\} Laplacian domain [-L, L]: f(t) \approx \sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j\, \phi_j(t)\, \sqrt{S(\lambda_j)}, S(\lambda) spectral density kernel evaluated frequency \lambda_j = j\pi/(2L), \beta_j \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} N(0,1). diseasenowcasting package uses M chosen automatically \lceil 1.5\sqrt{T}\rceil, capped 20 daily series longer 400 days avoid ill-conditioned Hessians.

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: f(t) = m_t, \quad m_t = \phi\, m_{t-1} + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim N(0, \sigma^2), stationary initialisation m_1 \sim N(0, \sigma^2 / (1 - \phi^2)).

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: mean incidence can instead derived compartmental model. familiar continuous-time SIR model splits population size N susceptible S, infectious , recovered R compartments, \frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta\,\frac{S\,}{N}, \qquad   \frac{dI}{dt} = \beta\,\frac{S\,}{N} - \gamma\, , \qquad   \frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma\, , transmission rate \beta recovery rate \gamma. use discrete-time version model, advancing one observation step time. Two standard adjustments make well behaved inference: track compartments fractions effective population N_{\text{eff}} = (S_0/N)\,N, writing s_t = S_t/N_{\text{eff}} i_t = I_t/N_{\text{eff}}. estimated susceptible fraction S_0/N lets model use effective population smaller census N. mass-action infection term \beta\,S\,/N replaced chain-binomial (Reed–Frost) survival form, number new infections can never exceed susceptible pool: \underbrace{\Delta_t}_{\text{new infections (fraction)}} = s_t\left(1 - e^{-\beta_t\, i_t}\right). compartments update exactly like SIR equations — susceptibles lose \Delta_t, infectious gain \Delta_t lose fraction \gamma recovery: s_{t+1} = s_t - \Delta_t, \qquad i_{t+1} = \Delta_t + (1-\gamma)\, i_t, mean reported incidence \mu_t = N_{\text{eff}}\,\Delta_t. transmission rate \beta_t = R_0\,\gamma\,e^{u_t} written terms basic reproduction number R_0 (since R_0 = \beta/\gamma), u_t follows AR(1) trend effective reproduction number R_t = R_0\,e^{u_t} drifts time. recovery rate \gamma\(0,1), basic reproduction number R_0, susceptible fraction S_0/N estimated parameters. Stratified (coupled) SIR: several strata, force infection stratum s depends total infectious pool \sum_{s'} i_t^{(s')}, \Delta_t^{(s)} = s_t^{(s)}\bigl(1 - e^{-\beta_t^{(s)} \sum_{s'} i_t^{(s')}}\bigr). captures cross-group transmission allowing stratum-specific transmission rates.

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: delay CDF G_D(\cdot \mid \theta) can : delay distribution constant time: single set parameters \theta governs G_D(\cdot \mid \theta) every event-time t.

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: Let L maximum explicitly modelled delay. probability simplex \{0, 1, \ldots, L\} receives Dirichlet prior: \bigl(g_D(0), \ldots, g_D(L)\bigr) \sim \mathrm{Dir}(\alpha_0, \ldots, \alpha_L). handle right-censoring (delays > L), (L+1)-th category captures tail probability. Conditional falling tail, delays follow \mathrm{Exponential}(1) distribution, yielding: \tilde{G}_D(x) = \begin{cases}   \sum_{k=0}^{\lfloor x\rfloor} \tilde{g}_D(k), & x \leq L, \\   \sum_{k=0}^{L} \tilde{g}_D(k)     + \tilde{g}_D(L+1)\bigl(1 - e^{-(x-(L+1))}\bigr), & x > L. \end{cases}

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: report date unavailable, corresponding delay right-censored analysis date \tau. contribution log-likelihood becomes \ln G_D(d^*_j \mid \theta_t) instead \ln \Delta G_D(d_j \mid \theta_t), yielding: \ell_t(\theta_t) = \ln\pi(\theta_t) + \sum_{\text{observed}} m_l \ln \Delta G_D(l \mid \theta_t) + \sum_{\text{censored}} m_j^* \ln G_D(j \mid \theta_t) + \ln S_k(\theta_t).

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: S strata, log-likelihood decomposes additively: \ell(\theta) = \sum_{t} \sum_{s=1}^{S} \ell_{t,s}(\theta), stratum contributes m_l^{(s)}, k^{(s)}, S_{k^{(s)}}(\theta) term. delay distribution G_D NB overdispersion \phi shared across strata; epidemic mean \mu_t^{(s)} GP/AR1/SIR trajectory parameters per-stratum.

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: diseasenowcasting performs inference via joint-mode Laplace approximation: Step 1 – MAP estimation. Minimise negative log-posterior -\ell(\theta) \text{ w.r.t. } \theta using nlminb (L-BFGS-B analytical gradients via RTMB/CppAD). Step 2 – Hessian. Evaluate H = -\nabla^2 \ell(\hat\theta) (precision matrix Laplace approximation) using RTMB’s automatic differentiation. Step 3 – Posterior draws. Sample \theta^{()} \sim N(\hat\theta,\; H^{-1}), \quad = 1, \ldots, B, via sparse Cholesky factorisation H. Step 4 – Predictive draws. \theta^{()}: \lambda_t^{()} = \exp\!\bigl(\text{cap}\bigl(\mu_t^{()}\bigr)\bigr), \quad G^*{}^{()}_t = G_D\!\bigl(d^*_t + 1 \mid \theta_t^{()}\bigr), \text{Nowcast}_t^{()} = k_t + \mathrm{NB}\!\bigl(\lambda_t^{()}(1 - G^*{}^{()}_t),\; \phi^{()}\bigr), \mathrm{cap}(\cdot) smooth upper-bound function preventing overflow: \text{cap}(x) = U - \log\!\bigl(1 + e^{U - x}\bigr), \quad U = \min(\max(6,\, \log(1 + k_{\max})),\, 16). posterior-predictive distribution nowcast event time t summarised draws.

title: Mathematical Foundations of diseasenowcasting
text: fit model two steps due non-identifiability delay epidemic processess. two-stage cascade addresses via multiple imputation: Stage 1. Fit delay-model recent window series (default: 120 events), obtaining posterior (\hat\theta_D, \hat\Sigma_D) delay parameters. Stage 2. Draw K delay parameter vectors \theta_D^{(1)}, \ldots, \theta_D^{(K)} spread around Stage-1 estimate, fix known constant, fit joint epidemic model . Pooling. Posterior-predictive draws pooled across imputations: \text{Pooled}_{t}^{()} = \text{Nowcast}_{t,\,k()}\!\left(\theta_D^{(k())}\right), k() cycles imputations.

Use HGSP long epidemic measurements 3+ months (data daily) 1 year (data weekly).

Sd very tight.      gamma = lognormal_prior(log(1/14), 0.1), # infectious ~ 14 days / tight sd     N_eff = beta_prior(5, 2),                # ~70% susceptible initially     N_pop = 50000000                         # ~population of Colombia   ),   delay = lognormal_delay(     mu    = normal_prior(log(5), 0.5),    # mean report delay ~5 days     sigma = gamma_prior(2, 0.5)           # delay SD   ) )

title: Nowcasting at the Start of an Epidemic

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: negative-binomial precision phi controls much count variability explained trend. single biggest driver interval width. Small precision phi results overdispersed process (wide intervals); large phi precise (tight intervals).  watch: phi’s main job width epidemic process (nowcast smoothed epidemic); barely moves delay (panel 1). 90% intervals routinely miss truth, use smaller phi (overdispersion); wide, use larger one.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: Delay parameters act first panel 1 (fitted delay distribution) propagate nowcast (panel 3), inflates -recent counts. Intuitively, delay heavier tail, nowcast wider interval past cases time arrive.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: mu (log) typical delay delay units (.e. delay usually takes 2 weeks \mu \approx log(14) units days \mu \approx log(2) units weeks). small mu implies reports arrive fast; large one, long delay (average).  watch: panel 1 slides left/right mu. fitted delay short nowcast -inflates recent weeks (panel 3 sits low); long -inflates.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: sigma (> 0) spread log-normal (.e. concentrated delays ). small sigma implies sharp spike typical delay; large value, heavy tail (_i.e. extreme values).  watch: sigma changes shape panel 1 without moving centre. heavier tail means cases still expected arrive, nowcast propagates uncertainty throughout recent past. shorter tail (smaller sigma) implies uncertainty concentrates close now nowcast.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: mu behaves similar Log-Normal location.  watch: Log-Normal, mu slides whole delay distribution (panel 1) shorter longer delays.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: sigma (> 0) controls spread around location.  watch: small sigma concentrates delay; large sigma disperses , widening right tail.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: Q interpolates tail. small value implies light tail; large value, heavy one.  watch: Q reshapes tail panel 1 leaving bulk roughly fixed. heavier tail makes late reports possible.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: Dirichlet delay learns free histogram. small value (< 1) lets spike delays; large one pushes towards assigning positive probability values.  watch: panel 1 shows effect. small alpha lets delay spike handful observed delays; large alpha lets delays possible. large value always susceptible noise.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: epidemic process smooth latent trend nowcast extrapolates. package offers three: flexible HSGP, simpler AR(1), mechanistic SIR.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: alpha Gaussian Process amplitude dictates far latent trend may swing. Small values imply flatter trend; larger lets squiggle .  watch: amplitude controls height latent trend (panel 2) therefore width nowcast (panel 3); delay (panel 1) untouched.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: ell GP length-scale (.e. horizontal “wavelength” trend). Small values imply wiggly quick bend epidemic process; large ones long smooth catching less variation.  watch: short value lets trend bend sharply peak (panel 2) cost wiggle; long one forces smoother curve can lag turning points.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: num_basis resolution GP. hyperparameter (number). default automatic set (~1.5\sqrt{T}) T maximum time planned observed.  watch: basis functions force -smooth trend can lag peak (panel 2); many let model track rapid change risk -fitting noise near end. delay (panel 1) unaffected.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: phi represents persistence autoregressive trend. Near 0 forgets past immediately goes back (log) mean epidemic; near 1 completely depends previous value.  watch: loose phi makes recent moves persist nowcast (panel 2-3); tight phi reverts mean quickly.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: sigma (> 0) size random step trend takes week. small value results smooth process large value jumpy.  watch: small sigma keeps trend smooth narrow intervals; large sigma lets wiggle, widening nowcast.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: R0 (> 0) sets fast mechanistic SIR epidemic grows. 1 extinguisges; large values grow extremely fast.  watch: R0 acts shape epidemic curve (panel 2) representing steeply climbs well final size epidemic.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: gamma (0, 1) rate people leave infectious pool (1/gamma represents mean infectious period). tight small value implies slow recovery, hence long epidemic individuals chance transmit. large loose value allows individuals recover fast hence reduce final-size epidemic.  watch: gamma sets epidemic’s duration speed decline (panel 2), shown weeks past peak.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: N_eff (0, 1) fraction population actually risk. small value tiny pool. epidemic saturates fast lower peak. large value implies longer epidemic individuals getting infected.  watch: N_eff sets size susceptible pool hence peak’s height (panel 2).

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: N_pop assumed total population. hyperparameter hence number.  watch: small N_pop saturates susceptible pool quickly (short, smaller peak); large one lets epidemic grow slowly time (longer, larger peak).

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: temporal effects (seasonality, day--week) attached nowcast() call, effect column becomes covariate. model(covariate_prior = ...) sets shared prior every covariate coefficient. fits use weekly dengue data 52-period seasonality attached.  watch: tight prior (near-zero coefficients) forces epidemic process explain variation alone. loose prior lets covariates pull curve strongly.

title: Understanding Priors and Hyperparameters in diseasenowcasting
text: set parameter model() can either two things: Pass number fix (constant prior), Pass *_prior() object give prior. See also summary beginning vignette specific steps.
code: # Fix values you know (numbers): mdl <- model(nb_likelihood(), hsgp_epidemic(num_basis = 5),              lognormal_delay(mu = log(2)))  # Or give priors (tight = confident, loose = diffuse): mdl <- model(nb_likelihood(phi = lognormal_prior(log(5), 0.5)),              hsgp_epidemic(alpha = half_normal_prior(0, 5)),              lognormal_delay(mu = normal_prior(log(7), 0.1)))  # Inspect what the defaults resolve to: default_priors(model(nb_likelihood(), hsgp_epidemic(), lognormal_delay()))

title: Introduction to diseasenowcasting: Real-Time Epidemic Nowcasting
text: diseasenowcasting works data organised tbl_now object companion tbl.now package. tbl_now simply data frame annotated roles columns: event date event happened (e.g. symptom onset) report date event reported (e.g. date entered database) strata (optional) columns defined strata (e.g. sex region) now (optional) date nowcast (assumes events reports now observed missing observations correspond observations - .e.  one day cases missingness can translated zero cases) quick example, build tbl_now using following surveillance data dengue Puerto Rico: can transform data.frame tbl_now specifying event report dates (onset report weeks respectively) well data_type strata (case, gender). data tbl_now, single call nowcast() rest. information tbl_now check package’s website.
code: library(diseasenowcasting) library(tbl.now) library(ggplot2) set.seed(27653) data(denguedat) #>   onset_week report_week gender #> 1 1990-01-01  1990-01-01   Male #> 2 1990-01-01  1990-01-01 Female #> 3 1990-01-01  1990-01-01 Female #> 4 1990-01-01  1990-01-08 Female #> 5 1990-01-01  1990-01-08   Male #> 6 1990-01-01  1990-01-15 Female dengue_tbl <- tbl_now(   denguedat,   event_date  = onset_week,    # symptom onset date   report_date = report_week,   # when the record was reported   data_type   = "linelist",    # another option is "count-incidence"  if data is aggregated   now         =  as.Date("1991-01-01") #When is the now of the nowcast ) dengue_tbl #> # A tibble:  52,987 × 6 #> # Data type: "linelist" #> # Frequency: Event: `weeks` | Report: `weeks` #>    onset_week   report_week   gender .event_num .report_num .delay #>    <date>       <date>        <chr>       <dbl>       <dbl>  <dbl> #>    [event_date] [report_date] [...]       [...]       [...]  [...] #>  1 1990-01-01   1990-01-01    Male            0           0      0 #>  2 1990-01-01   1990-01-01    Female          0           0      0 #>  3 1990-01-01   1990-01-01    Female          0           0      0 #>  4 1990-01-01   1990-01-08    Female          0           1      1 #>  5 1990-01-01   1990-01-08    Male            0           1      1 #>  6 1990-01-01   1990-01-15    Female          0           2      2 #>  7 1990-01-01   1990-01-15    Female          0           2      2 #>  8 1990-01-01   1990-01-15    Female          0           2      2 #>  9 1990-01-01   1990-01-22    Female          0           3      3 #> 10 1990-01-01   1990-01-08    Female          0           1      1 #> # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> # Now: 1991-01-01 | Event date: "onset_week" | Report date: "report_week" #> # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> # ℹ 52,977 more rows

what: Example 3 – Comparing models with a backtest
title: Introduction to diseasenowcasting: Real-Time Epidemic Nowcasting
text: backtest reruns nowcasts multiple historical dates scores eventually-observed totals. lets compare models committing one real-time monitoring. backtest() function fits one nowcast per date model cell. cells run parallel future framework. default run sequentially; use several CPU cores, set parallel plan calling backtest(): follows run backtest sequential mode however strongly recommend using many workers multisession plan possible: can plot Weighted Interval Score (WIS) interval coverage:  obtain via score(): score() output shows WIS, absolute percentage error (APE), empirical coverage 50 % 90 %. well-calibrated nowcast lowest WIS coverage close levels. specific test choose SIR lowest WIS essentially coverage HGSP. Note however tutorial used 5 historical dates low reach definite conslusion.
code: library(future) plan(multisession, workers = 4)   # 4 parallel R sessions # ... run backtest() ... plan(sequential)                  # restore serial execution when done # Compare HSGP (flexible GP trend) vs AR1 (autoregressive trend)  # and SIR (susceptible, infected, recovered) on mpox models_to_compare <- list(   model(nb_likelihood(), hsgp_epidemic(), lognormal_delay()),   model(nb_likelihood(), ar1_epidemic(),  lognormal_delay()),   model(nb_likelihood(), sir_epidemic(),  lognormal_delay()) )  #Uncomment this line to use several of your cores #plan(multisession, workers = 4)   backtest_mpox <- backtest(   mpox_tbl,   models  = models_to_compare,   n_dates = 5 #Test 3 dates at random from the mpox data )  #This closes the plan multisession opened above #plan(sequential) # Plot scoring metrics autoplot(backtest_mpox) # Rank by Weighted Interval Score (WIS) -- lower is better score(backtest_mpox) #>               model       wis overprediction underprediction dispersion #> 1  SIR/nb/LogNormal  9.811354       0.000000        6.855556   2.955799 #> 2  AR1/nb/LogNormal 13.368333       3.166667        2.500000   7.701667 #> 3 HSGP/nb/LogNormal 17.360035       2.708333        4.402778  10.248924 #>   coverage_50 coverage_90       ape      mse n #> 1        0.75           1 0.4073142 1660.250 4 #> 2        0.25           1 7.1411720 1260.500 4 #> 3        0.50           1 1.6262153 2064.062 4

title: diseasenowcasting — Bayesian Epidemic Nowcasting
text: Surveillance systems delay true date event (event_date, e.g. symptom onset testing) report date event (report_date, e.g. case entered database). recent event-dates still missing reports yet arrived, latest case counts look artificially low. nowcast corrects reporting delay estimates eventual, fully-observed totals. diseasenowcasting fits censored Bayesian models. reporting delay modeled directly stochastic process, jointly epidemic dynamics, censored likelihood. Inference runs R’s Template Model Builder (RTMB), Stan/JAGS compilation required. package supports several epidemic processes, several delay families, stratified data, extreme value detection backtesting. ⚠️ diseasenowcasting currently active development parts interface might still change.

title: diseasenowcasting — Bayesian Epidemic Nowcasting
text: diseasenowcasting works data organised tbl_now object companion tbl.now package: data frame annotated roles columns (event date, report date, optionally strata now date everything observed). annotate data tbl_now(), pointing event report dates (symptom-onset report weeks) strata (gender): data tbl_now(), single call nowcast() rest (nowcast automatically stratified strata declared ): Results can visualised autoplot() (bars show median, error bars 90% credible interval) extracted predict() / summary(): Nowcast dengue example, gender. can choose different epidemic process, delay family likelihood passing model() nowcast(): backtest score evaluate different models performed past get scores can access quantities directly score
code: set.seed(6728) library(diseasenowcasting) library(tbl.now) library(dplyr)  # Example surveillance data (dengue in Puerto Rico) shipped with tbl.now data(denguedat)  #Simulate a nowcast on January 1st 1991 #Only cases and reports before that date will be used dengue_data <- denguedat |>    filter(onset_week <= as.Date("1991-01-01") & report_week <= as.Date("1991-01-01")) dengue_tbl <- tbl_now(   dengue_data,   event_date  = onset_week,    # when symptoms started   report_date = report_week,   # when the case was registered   strata      = gender,   data_type   = "linelist",    # use "count-incidence" for pre-aggregated counts   now         = as.Date("1991-01-01"),   t_effects   = temporal_effects(seasons = 52) #Weekly seasonal effect (Fourier)   ) ncast <- nowcast(dengue_tbl, n_draws = 1000) autoplot(ncast) pred <- predict(ncast)   # full posterior-predictive nowcast at every event-time summary(pred) #> # A tibble: 6 × 16 #>    mean median    sd   mad  q2.5    q5   q10   q25   q50   q75   q90   q95 q97.5 #>   <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 109.     108  2.09  1.48   107   107   107   107   108   110 112     113 114   #> 2  89.3     89  3.10  2.97    86    86    86    87    89    90  93      95  97   #> 3  69.0     68  5.74  4.45    62    63    64    65    68    71  76      79  82.0 #> 4  48.7     45 13.7   8.90    35    36    37    40    45    53  65      73  83.0 #> 5  47.8     41 26.3  17.8     22    23    26    31    41    56  77.1    95 111.  #> 6  43.1     35 29.3  22.2     11    13    17    23    35    55  79.1    98 116   #> # ℹ 3 more variables: .event_num <int>, stratum <chr>, event_date <date> model_1 <- model(   likelihood = nb_likelihood(),          # negative binomial (recommended)   epidemic   = ar1_epidemic(),           # AR(1) process    delay      = generalized_gamma_delay() # generalized gamma reporting delay ) nowcast(dengue_tbl, model = model_1) model_2 <- model(   likelihood = nb_likelihood(),  # negative binomial (recommended)   epidemic   = hsgp_epidemic(),  # recommended for long-running epidemics   delay      = lognormal_delay() # generalized gamma reporting delay )  #Set ndates to the number of dates you want for the backtest bt <- backtest(dengue_tbl, list(model_1, model_2), n_dates = 3) autoplot(bt) score(bt) #>                     model      wis overprediction underprediction dispersion #> 1 AR1/nb/GeneralizedGamma 12.10694              0        8.592593   3.514352 #> 2       HSGP/nb/LogNormal 12.30653              0        6.777778   5.528750 #>   coverage_50 coverage_90       ape  mse n #> 1   0.0000000           1 0.7404989 1342 3 #> 2   0.3333333           1 0.7030839 1089 3

title: diseasenowcasting — Bayesian Epidemic Nowcasting
text: new data arrives, update() re-scores every incoming report previously fitted delay distribution warns anything seems implausibly delayed. flagged reports available via extreme_values(): complete tutorial handling extreme delays available Handling Outlier Delays Censoring vignette.
code: #Simulate getting new data dengue_update <- denguedat |>    filter(onset_week <= as.Date("1991-01-14") & report_week <= as.Date("1991-01-14"))  # level stands for when should we flag something is an extreme delay # level = 0.99 means delays with probability 1  - 0.99 = 0.01 are considered extreme nc_updated <- update(ncast, new_data = dengue_update, level = 0.99)  # The model identified two extreme (very unlikely) delays of 9 and 10 weeks:  # A human should check them based on domain knowledge and decide whether to keep # them or to censor them.  extreme_values(nc_updated)

title: Backtest one or more nowcast models across a set of as-of dates — backtest
text: data tbl_now (full data; eventual counts truth). models model() object list .  several models backtest can rank (see score()). dates vector -dates.  NULL, default grid spanning observed range (interior points) sampled. type "two_stage" (default) "one_stage". n_dates dates NULL, many sample. Default 20. max_delay Truth-completeness horizon (event units).  Event dates within max_delay units last report yet fully observed eventual count, backtest excludes ("truth" still accruing).  Default NULL uses 99th percentile observed reporting delays.  Pass number override, Inf evaluate every date regardless completeness. return_simulations TRUE, also keep pooled draw matrix per (date, model).  Default FALSE (summaries : mean/median/sd/quantiles). n_draws Posterior draws per nowcast. K, np_spread Two-stage controls passed . seed Optional base RNG seed. ... Passed nowcast().

code: lognormal_delay(   mu = numeric(0),   sigma = numeric(0),   num_delay_seasons = 1L,   season_distribution = std_normal_prior() )  gamma_delay(   shape = numeric(0),   rate = numeric(0),   num_delay_seasons = 1L,   season_distribution = std_normal_prior() )  generalized_gamma_delay(   mu = numeric(0),   sigma = numeric(0),   Q = numeric(0),   num_delay_seasons = 1L,   season_distribution = std_normal_prior() )  dirichlet_delay(alpha = numeric(0), bins = numeric(0))

title: Delay distribution for the Bayesian Nowcast — delay_process
text: mu Log-mean intercept (delay_mu). sigma Log-scale / SD parameter > 0. num_delay_seasons Number periodic delay seasons. Default 1. season_distribution Prior delay-season effects. shape, rate Gamma delay parameters (shape slot log-mean, rate slot delay SD; see original parameterisation). Q GenGamma shape (delay_Q); Q = 0 recovers lognormal. alpha Dirichlet concentration (scalar broadcast bins). bins Dirichlet: number explicit delay bins (geometric tail beyond).

title: Delay distribution for the Bayesian Nowcast — delay_process
text: delay_process_class object.

title: Delay distribution for the Bayesian Nowcast — delay_process
text: prior argument left empty, default_priors() supplies defaults.  delay mean prior data-informed: normal_prior() log scale, centred log median observed delay. LogNormal: mu ~ data-informed normal_prior(log median delay); sigma ~ gamma_prior(2, 2). Gamma: shape ~ data-informed normal_prior(...); rate ~ gamma_prior(2, 2 / sd) (data-informed SD). Generalised Gamma: mu ~ data-informed normal_prior(...); sigma ~ gamma_prior(2, 0.1); Q (shape) ~ normal_prior(0, 0.5). Dirichlet: alpha ~ per-bin concentration vector, data-informed empirical delay pmf (0.05 + (bins+1) * pmf), else rep(1, bins + 1).

title: Delay distribution for the Bayesian Nowcast — delay_process
code: lognormal_delay() #> LogNormal(mu, sigma) lognormal_delay(mu = normal_prior(log(7), 0.5)) #> LogNormal(mu ~ Normal(1.946, 0.500), sigma) gamma_delay() #> Gamma(shape, rate) generalized_gamma_delay(Q = 1) #> GeneralizedGamma(mu, sigma, Q = "1") dirichlet_delay(alpha = 1, bins = 21) #> Dirichlet(alpha = "1"; bins = "21")

what: diseasenowcasting: Nowcast Time Series of Epidemiological Cases — diseasenowcasting-package
title: diseasenowcasting: Nowcast Time Series of Epidemiological Cases — diseasenowcasting-package
text: Implements Bayesian nowcasting models incident disease cases using censored point-process approach. reporting delay modelled directly stochastic process, jointly epidemic dynamics, via censored likelihood. Inference uses RTMB (CppAD autodiff + Laplace approximation). Supports multiple epidemic models (HSGP, AR1, SIR), multiple delay families (LogNormal, GenGamma, Dirichlet), stratified data, extreme-delay detection, backtesting.

Default: lognormal_delay(). covariate_prior prior_class applied covariate coefficients. Default: std_normal_prior(). strata_pooling "independent" (default) fits fully separate intercepts per stratum.  "hierarchical" pools intercepts via shared mean half-normal prior -stratum SD: \(\mu_0^{(s)} = \mu_{\text{global}} + \tau \cdot \delta^{(s)}\), \(\delta^{(s)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\), \(\tau \sim \text{HalfNormal}(0,1)\). relevant num_strata > 1.

Default: model() (NB + HSGP + Dirichlet). type "two_stage" (default; delay-imputation pooling) "one_stage" (single joint fit). now -date; events/reports now used.  Default: tbl.now::get_now(data), falling back latest report date. K Number delay imputations two-stage path. n_draws Default number posterior draws used predict() latent-incidence summaries. delay_window Recent window length parametric Stage-1 delay fit. np_spread Dirichlet simplex imputation covariance inflation (default 1). floor_mu, floor_sig_frac Imputation-spread floors (parametric families). temporal_effects Controls automatic seasonal / day--week covariates. "auto" (default) adds sensible effects based data's time unit (weekly -> 52-period seasonality; daily -> day--week + 52-period seasonality; monthly -> 12-period seasonality) tbl_now already carry computed temporal effects.  Use "none" ("None") disable, pre-attach effects tbl_now tbl.now::add_temporal_effects() + tbl.now::compute_temporal_effects(). prior_only TRUE, ignore likelihood draw epidemic parameters priors , returning prior-predictive latent incidence.  Useful understanding prior implies seeing data (e.g. SIR R0 prior AR(1) phi prior reshapes epidemic).  result normal nowcast_class, predict() / autoplot() / median() / quantile() work; data supplies time grid. text: Given fitted nowcast new data arrived fit, compute surprising new data fitted posterior. Two types surprise supported (select via type argument): "count": surprising new case count given event time "delay": surprising report long delay "": Compute (requires appropriate new_data format).

title: Compute surprise scores for new observations — surprise

title: Compute surprise scores for new observations — surprise
text: object nowcast_class object (fit() result). new_data type = "count": data.frame columns event_index (0-indexed) count (observed new total time). type = "delay": data.frame columns delay (numeric delay values) optionally weight (counts delay, default 1). type = "": supply sets columns. type surprise type(s) compute. One "count", "delay", "" (default ""). level Credible level used flag surprises (default 0.99).  count flagged falls outer (1 - level)/2 tail posterior predictive (high low); delay flagged P(D >= d) P(D <= d) 1 - level (surprisingly long short). n_draws Number posterior draws (default 500). seed Optional RNG seed.

title: Compute surprise scores for new observations — surprise
text: list : $count_surprise (data.frame): one row per event time event_index, observed, posterior_mean, lpd, ppp_right, ppp_left, relative_surprise. $delay_surprise (data.frame): one row per delay value delay, tail_prob (= 1 - G_D(delay)), lpd, relative_surprise.
